Fortunately, the integrals on the right-hand sides of the equations of motion may be approximated using only the masses and the three principal moments of inertia of Earth and Moon. A typical denominator in the equations of motion has the general form

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { | \pmb { \varDelta } - \boldsymbol { r } | } } , \quad \mathrm { w h e r e } \quad | \pmb { \varDelta } | = \Delta \ll { r } = | \boldsymbol { r } | .
$$

This quantity may be developed into a rapidly convergent series (se, e.g, [25])

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { | \mathbf { A } - \mathbf { r } | } } = { \frac { 1 } { r } } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } \left( { \frac { \varDelta } { r } } \right) ^ { n } P _ { n } \left( { \frac { r \cdot \mathbf { A } } { r \varDelta } } \right) = { \frac { 1 } { r } } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } \left( { \frac { \varDelta } { r } } \right) ^ { n } P _ { n } ( \cos \phi ) \ ,
$$

using the smallquantity  as the argument of the development. \$ is the angle between the vectors r and A, and Pn(x) are Legendre polynomials (named after Adrien Marie Legendre (1752-1833)) of degree n:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { P _ { 0 } ( x ) = 1 } \\ { P _ { 1 } ( x ) = x } \\ { P _ { 2 } ( x ) = \frac { 3 } { 2 } x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \\ { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \cdot } \end{array}
$$

In view of the fact that the distances between Earth, Moon and Sun are large compared to the physical sizes of Earth and Moon, we may confine ourselves to the approximation up to terms of order $n = 2$ in the above series development:

$$
\frac { 1 } { | \Delta - r | } = \frac { 1 } { r } \left\{ 1 + \frac { r \cdot \Delta } { r ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \Delta ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( r \cdot \Delta ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 4 } } \right\} .
$$

We will also need the third power of the above quantities:

$$
\frac { 1 } { | \Delta - r | ^ { 3 } } = \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \left\{ 1 + 3 \frac { r \cdot \Delta } { r ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \Delta ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 5 } { 2 } \frac { ( r \cdot \Delta ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 4 } } \right\} .
$$

The integrals have to be evaluated in a well-defined coordinate system. Eventually, we may need the accelerations in the inertial system. It is much simpler, however, to perform the integration in the body-fixed systems, the terms involving integrals over $V _ { \xi }$ in the Earth-fixed, the terms with integrals over $V _ { \zeta }$ in the Moon-fixed system. Afterwards. the accelerations are transformed back into the inertial system using the transformation matrices $\mathbf { r } _ { \delta }$ or $\mathbf { \Delta } ^ { \prime } \mathbf { r } _ { \mathbb { Q } }$

Let us first deal with the equations (3.85) for the orbital motions of the Moon and the Sun. The Sun is treated as a point mass in these equations, which is why the structure of the equation for the Sun seems slightly simpler than that for the Moon. Taking into account the development (3.1o2) we may approximate the first integral for the equation of motion for the Sun on the right-hand side as

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \int _ { \gamma _ { \odot } } \frac { \rho _ { p _ { r } } } { | r _ { \odot } - r _ { p } | } d V _ { \delta } = \int _ { V _ { \odot } } ^ { \rho _ { p _ { r } } } \{ 1 + \frac { r _ { \odot } \cdot r _ { p } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { r _ { p } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( r _ { \odot } \cdot r _ { p } ) ^ { 2 } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 4 } } \} d V _ { \odot } } } \\ & { } & { = \frac { 1 } { r _ { \odot } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r ^ { 2 } d V _ { \ S } \qquad \mathrm { ( 3 . 1 6 ) } } \\ & { } & { + \frac { 3 } { 2 r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } \{ r _ { \odot _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + r _ { \odot _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + r _ { \odot _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \} d V _ { \delta } . } \end{array}
$$

$\frac { 1 } { r _ { \odot } }$ (dueto the defntion of $\rho _ { p _ { r } }$ ）， the second vanishes because the body-fixed system has the origin in the center of mass. Using the definitions for the principal moments of inertia (3.79) and taking into account the relations (3.80), the above approximation assumes the form

$$
\int _ { V _ { \odot } } \frac { \rho _ { p _ { r } } } { | r _ { \odot } - r _ { p } | } ~ d V _ { \Game } = \frac { 1 } { r _ { \odot } } ~ + ~ \frac { A _ { \delta } + B _ { \delta } ~ + C _ { \delta } } { 2 M r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } ~ - ~ \frac { 3 ~ \left( r _ { \odot _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \delta } ~ + ~ r _ { \odot _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \delta } ~ + ~ r _ { \odot _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \delta } \right) } { 2 M r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } }
$$

Formula (3.105) may be brought into a standard form by

$$
I _ { e _ { \odot } } = e _ { \odot _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \Vec { \diamond } } + e _ { \odot _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \Vec { \diamond } } + e _ { \odot _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \Vec { \diamond } } .
$$

$I _ { e _ { \odot } }$ is the moment of inertia of the Earth in the direction $e _ { ( \cdot ) }$ , the geocentric unit vector to the Sun. Using this result in eqn. (3.105) we obtain:

$$
\int _ { { V _ { \odot } } } \frac { \rho _ { p _ { r } } } { | r _ { \odot } - r _ { p } | } d V _ { \odot } = \frac { 1 } { r _ { \odot } } + \frac { A _ { \ S } + B _ { \delta } + C _ { \delta } - 3 I _ { e _ { \odot } } } { 2 M r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } .
$$

Because the principal moments of inertia of the Earth have very similar numerical values (see Table II- 2.1), the moment of inertia in direction $e _ { \odot }$ will not differ much from the three principal moments of inertia. As a consequence, $A _ { \delta } + B _ { \delta } + C _ { \delta } - 3 I _ { e _ { \odot } }$ will be a small quantity, of the order of the differences between the principal moments of inertia. Formula (3.107).after multiplication with $\textit { G M }$ , represents the potential function of the attractive force exerted by the mass distribution ( $V _ { ☉ }$ and $\rho _ { p }$ ) on a point mass with barycentric position vector $r _ { \odot }$ . The formula is known as MacCullagh's formula due to James MacCullagh (1809-1847).

Formulas (3.105) or (3.107), when written in this form, have to be evaluated in the Earth-fixed system. This circumstance has to be observed when taking the gradient of this potential function (as required in the equations of motion). Although it would be possible to develop formulae for performing these operations directly in the inertial system (see, e.g., [14]) we prefer to use the following formulation based on taking the gradient in the Earth-fixed system and then to transform the result back into the inertial system using matrix $\mathbf { T } _ { \mathtt { d } } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \mathbf { T } _ { \mathtt { d } } ( \varPsi _ { \mathtt { d } } , \varepsilon _ { \mathtt { d } } , \varTheta _ { \mathtt { d } } )$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \nabla _ { \odot } \int \frac { \rho _ { p _ { r } } } { | \boldsymbol { r } _ { \odot } - \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } | } \ d V _ { \delta } = \mathbf { T } _ { \delta } \nabla _ { \odot } \{ \frac { 1 } { r _ { \odot } } + \frac { A _ { \delta } + B _ { \delta } + C _ { \delta } } { 2 M r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } }  } } \\ & { } & { \qquad \quad -  \frac { 3 \ ( r _ { \odot _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \delta } + r _ { \odot _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \delta } + r _ { \odot _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \delta } ) } { 2 M r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } \} . } \end{array}
$$

All component matrices and the gradient on the left-hand side refer to the inertial, all symbols on the right-hand side to the Earth-fixed system.

The result (3.108) may be transcribed easily to the second term in the equation of motion for the Sun in eqns. (3.85): the geocentric position vector $r _ { \odot }$ of the Sun has to be replaced by the selenocentric position vector $r _ { \mathbb { C } \odot } \ { \stackrel { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } } \ r _ { \odot } - r _ { \mathbb { C } }$ ，and the Earth-related quantities (mass, density, volume) have to be replaced by the corresponding Moon-related quantities:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle \nabla _ { \mathbb { Q } } \int _ { V _ { \mathbb { Q } } } \frac { \rho _ { \wp r } } { | r _ { \mathbb { Q } \odot } - r _ { \mathbb { Q } \wp } | } ~ d V _ { \mathbb { q } } = \mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ~ \nabla _ { \mathbb { Q } } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { r _ { \mathbb { Q } \odot } } + \frac { A _ { \mathbb { Q } } + B _ { \mathbb { Q } } + C _ { \mathbb { Q } } } { 2 m _ { \mathbb { Q } } r _ { \mathbb { Q } \odot } ^ { 3 } } \right. } & { } \\ { \displaystyle \left. - \frac { 3 ~ \left( r _ { \mathbb { Q } \odot _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \hat { \otimes } } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \odot _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \hat { \otimes } } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \odot _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \hat { \ S } } \right) } { 2 m _ { \mathbb { Q } } ~ r _ { \mathbb { Q } \odot } ^ { 5 } } \right\} } & { . } \end{array}
$$

The last term to be considered in the equations of motion for the Sun (3.85) contains a double integration over the volumes occupied by Earth and Moon. First,the potential function of this term is considered. Equation (3.103) has to be used to approximate the denominator on the left-hand side. In an attempt to reduce the formalism we put

$$
\pmb { \Delta } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } r _ { p } - r _ { \mathbb { S } \wp }
$$

and obtain (observe the sign to be chosen to be consistent with formula (3.103):

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \int _ { \mathcal { T } _ { \mathcal { G } } } \int _ { \rho _ { b r } } \rho _ { p _ { r } } \frac { \left( \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathcal { G } } + \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathcal { G } , \flat } - \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } \right) \cdot \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathcal { G } } } { | \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathcal { G } } + \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathcal { G } \flat } - \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } | ^ { 3 } } d V _ { \delta } d V _ { \mathbb { G } } = \displaystyle \int _ { V _ { \mathcal { G } } } \int \frac { \rho _ { b r } \rho _ { r } } { r _ { \mathcal { G } } ^ { 3 } } \left\{ 1 + \frac { 3 } { r _ { \mathcal { G } } ^ { 2 } } \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathcal { G } } \cdot \boldsymbol { \varDelta } \right. } \\ & { \left. - \frac { 3 } { 2 r _ { \mathcal { G } } ^ { 2 } } \boldsymbol { \varDelta } ^ { 2 } \ + \frac { 1 5 } { 2 r _ { \mathcal { G } } ^ { 4 } } \left( \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathbb { G } } \cdot \boldsymbol { \varDelta } \right) ^ { 2 } \right\} \left( \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathbb { G } } - \boldsymbol { \varDelta } \right) \cdot \boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathbb { G } } d V _ { \delta } d V _ { \mathbb { G } } \ . } \end{array}
$$

All terms in the brackets $\{ \cdot \cdot \cdot \}$ , except for the second, give non-zero contributions when multiplied with $r _ { \mathbb { C } } ~ { \cdot } ~ r _ { \mathbb { C } } ~ ,$ only the second term contributes (considering only terins up to the second order in △) when multiplied with Δ · ro . Assuming again that we evaluate the integrals either in the Earthfixed or the Moon-fixed systems and considering that all terins linear in the components of either $r _ { p }$ or $r _ { \mathbb { ( } \wp _ { \delta } }$ and the mixed terms $r _  p \} ~ r _ { p \ k }$ will give no contribution after integration, the following terms actually have to be considered of the above expressions (all other terms are marked with .. . in the following equations):

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \varDelta ^ { 2 } } & { \qquad = r _ { p } ^ { 2 } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \wp } ^ { 2 } - \dots } \\ { ( r _ { \mathbb { Q } } \cdot \varDelta ) ^ { 2 } } & { \qquad = r _ { p 1 } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } + r _ { p _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } + r _ { p _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } + \dots } \\ & { \qquad \cdot + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \wp _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \wp _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \wp _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } + \dots } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left( r _ { \mathbb { Q } } \cdot { \\varDelta } \right) \left( r _ { \odot } \cdot { \varDelta } \right) = r _ { p _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 1 } } r _ { \odot _ { 1 } } + r _ { p _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 2 } } r _ { \odot _ { 2 } } + r _ { p _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 3 } } r _ { \odot _ { 3 } } + \ldots } \\ & { \qquad + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \wp _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 1 } } r _ { \odot _ { 1 } } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \wp _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 2 } } r _ { \odot _ { 2 } } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \wp _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 3 } } r _ { \odot _ { 3 } } + \ldots ~ . . . ~ . } \end{array}
$$

The relevant terms related to Earth and Moon are very nicely separated. Note that the components of the vectors $r _ { \mathbb { ( } }$ and $r _ { \odot }$ are referred to the geocentric PAI-system, to the corresponding selenocentric PAI-system for Noon-related parts. Using the expressions (3.112) we may give eqns. (3.11) the following explicit form:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \int _ { \mathcal { C } _ { \xi } } \displaystyle \int _ { \rho _ { \xi } , \rho _ { \xi } } \left( r _ { \xi } + r _ { \xi ( \psi - r _ { \psi } ) } \cdot r _ { \xi } \right. } \\ & { \left. \mathrm { ~ \ } _ { \psi _ { \xi } } ^ { 2 } \cdot r _ { \xi } \right) } \\ & { \displaystyle + \left\{ \frac { 3 } { 2 r _ { \xi } ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \dot { N } _ { l } } \left( A _ { \xi } + B _ { \xi } + C _ { \xi } \right) - \frac { 1 5 } { 2 r _ { \xi } ^ { \frac { 7 } { 2 } } M } \left( r _ { \xi _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \delta } + r _ { \xi _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \delta } + r _ { \xi _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \xi } \right) \right\} r _ { \xi } \cdot r _ { \xi } } \\ & { \displaystyle - \frac { 3 } { r _ { \xi } ^ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } M } \left\{ A _ { \xi } r _ { \xi } r _ { \Theta } _ { \xi } + B _ { \xi } r _ { \xi _ { 2 } } r _ { \phi _ { 2 } } + C _ { \xi } r _ { \xi _ { 3 } } r _ { \phi _ { 3 } } \right\} } \\ & { \displaystyle + \left\{ \frac { 3 } { 2 r _ { \xi } ^ { \frac { 9 } { 2 } } m } \left( A _ { \xi } + B _ { \xi } + C _ { \xi } \right) - \frac { 1 5 } { 2 r _ { \xi } ^ { \frac { 7 } { 2 } } m } \left( r _ { \xi _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \xi } + r _ { \xi _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \xi } + r _ { \xi _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \xi } \right) \right\} r _ { \xi } \cdot r _ { \xi } } \\ & { \displaystyle - \frac { 3 } { r _ { \xi } ^ { \frac { 9 } { 2 } } m } \left\{ A _ { \xi } r _ { \xi _ { 1 } } r _ { \phi _ { 1 } } + B _ { \xi } r _ { \xi _ { 2 } } r _ { \phi _ { 2 } } + C _ { \xi } r _ { \xi _ { 3 } } r _ { \phi _ { 3 } } \right\} . } \end{array}
$$

Note that for A = B = Cg and Aq = Bg = Cq the above scalar function is reduced to the first term on the right-hand side. which is. as a matter of fact, the term of the classical three-body problem. Using matrix notation, the gradient of eqn. (3.113) may be written in the following elegant way:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \nabla _ { \mathbb { Q } } \displaystyle \int _ { V _ { \mathbb { Q } } } \displaystyle \int _ { \rho _ { \varepsilon } } \rho _ { p _ { r } } \frac { \left( r _ { \mathbb { Q } } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \rho } - r _ { p } \right) \cdot r _ { \mathbb { Q } } } { | r _ { \mathbb { Q } } + r _ { \mathbb { Q } \rho } - r _ { p } | ^ { 3 } } d V _ { \mathbb { A } } d V _ { \mathbb { G } } = \frac { 1 } { r _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 3 } } r _ { \mathbb { Q } } } \\ & { + \frac { 3 } { 2 r _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 5 } M } \mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { S } } \left\{ \left[ \left( A _ { \mathbb { S } } + B _ { \mathbb { S } } + C _ { \mathbb { S } } - \frac { 5 } { r _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 2 } } r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { T } \mathbf { I } _ { \mathbb { S } , \ r _ { \mathbb { Q } } } \right) \mathbf { E } + 2 \mathbf { I } _ { \mathbb { S } , \ r } \right] r _ { \mathbb { G } } \right\} } \\ & { + \frac { 3 } { 2 r _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 5 } m } \mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { Q } } \left\{ \left[ \left( A _ { \mathbb { Q } } + B _ { \mathbb { Q } } + C _ { \mathbb { Q } } - \frac { 5 } { r _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 2 } } r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { T } \mathbf { I } _ { \mathbb { Q } , \ r } \right) \mathbf { E } + 2 \mathbf { I } _ { \mathbb { Q } , \ r } \right] r _ { \mathbb { Q } } \right\} . } \end{array}
$$

Equation (3.114) refers to the inertial system. The first bracket on the righthand side has to be evaluated in the geocentric. Earth-fixed PAl-system, the second bracket in the corresponding selenocentric system.

With eqns. (3.108), (3.109) and (3.114) we may approximate the equations of motion for the Sun in the three-body problem using the masses and the principal moments of inertia. Using the work which was necessary to perform this task it is now comparatively easy to find the corresponding approximation for the motion of the Moon in eqns. (3.85). Let us first compute the two-body term of this motion:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \displaystyle \int _ { V _ { \mathbb { G } } } \displaystyle \int _ { \left. r _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } \rho } - r _ { p } \right. } d V _ { \delta } \ d V _ { \mathbb { G } } = \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } } \right\} } \\ & { + \mathbf { T } _ { \delta } \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \left\{ \frac { A _ { \delta } + B _ { \delta } + C _ { \delta } } { 2 M r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 \left( r _ { \mathbb { G } _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \delta } + r _ { \mathbb { G } _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \delta } + r _ { \mathbb { G } _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \delta } \right) } { 2 M r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 5 } } \right\} } \\ & { + \mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { G } } \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \left\{ \frac { A _ { \mathbb { G } } + B _ { \mathbb { G } } + C _ { \mathbb { G } } } { 2 m _ { \mathbb { G } } r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 \left( r _ { \mathbb { G } _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \mathbb { G } } \right) } { 2 m _ { \mathbb { G } } r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 5 } } \right\} . } \end{array}
$$

The first bracket on the right-hand side may be directly evaluated in the inertial system, the second refers to the geocentric.the last to the selenocentric PAI-systems.

Equation (3.115). after multiplication with G(A1 + m@ ). contains the potential function for the generalized two-body problem Earth-Moon. The relative motion of the centers of mass of two bodies of arbitrary shape is thus de-seribed by a potential fumction which is the "weighted" sum of the potential

functions as given by MacCullagh's formulas for the Earth and the Moon $\frac { G ( M + m _ { \mathbb { Q } } ) } { M }$ $\frac { G ( M + m _ { \mathbb { Q } } ) } { m _ { \mathbb { Q } } }$   
e.g., reported by Brouwer and Clemence [27].

The first part of the perturbation acceleration in the equations of motion for the Moon is in essence already given by formula (3.109):

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \displaystyle \int _ { V _ { \mathbb { G } } } \frac { \rho _ { \wp r } } { | r _ { \mathbb { G } \odot } - r _ { \mathbb { G } \wp } | } d V _ { \mathbb { G } } = } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \displaystyle \mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { G } } \nabla _ { 1 } A _ { \delta } + r _ { \mathbb { G } \odot _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \delta } + r _ { \mathbb { G } \odot _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \delta } ) } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \end{array}
$$

and the indirect part of the perturbative acceleration may be transcribed from eqn. (3.114):

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \nabla _ { \mathbb { Q } } \displaystyle \int _ { V _ { \odot } } \rho _ { p _ { r } } \frac { ( r _ { \odot } - r _ { p } ) \cdot r _ { \odot } } { | r _ { \odot } \cdot - r _ { p } | ^ { 3 } } ~ d V _ { \odot } = \frac { 1 } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } r _ { \odot } } \\ & { + \displaystyle \frac { 3 } { 2 r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } M } \mathbf { T } _ { \odot } \left\{ \left[ \left( A _ { \delta } + B _ { \delta } + C _ { \delta } - \frac { 5 } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 2 } } r _ { \odot } ^ { T } \mathbf { I } _ { \delta , \ F } r _ { \odot } \right) \mathbf { E } + 2 \mathbf { I } _ { \delta , \ F } \right] r _ { \odot } \right\} . } \end{array}
$$

With this result the equations of motion for the generalized three-body problem may be summarized in the approxiination sought in this section. The terms below are arranged to let the point mass approximation precede the terms proportional to the principal moments of inertia. The latter terins are expressed as gradients of brackets $\{ \cdot \cdot \cdot \}$ . The gradients have to be evaluated in the corresponding body-fixed PAI-system. Afterwards the term is trans-formed into the inertial system using the matrices $\mathbf { T } _ { \textrm { f } }$ and $\mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { G } } .$ When solving the equations of motion with numerical techniques it is thus necessary to transform the geocentric position vectors of Sun and Moon at each integra-tion step into the geocentric and the selenocentric PAI-system, to evaluate the gradients in these systems,and to transform the result back into the inertial system.

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \bar { r } _ { \mathbb { G } } = - G ( M + m _ { \mathbb { G } } ) \frac { r _ { \mathbb { G } } } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 3 } } - G m _ { \mathcal { G } } [ \frac { r _ { \mathbb { G } } - r _ { \mathbb { G } } } { | r _ { \mathbb { G } } - r _ { \mathbb { G } } | ^ { 3 } } + \frac { r _ { 0 } } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 3 } } ] } & { } \\ & { \quad \quad + \frac { G ( M + m _ { \mathbb { G } } ) } { 2 M } \mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { G } } \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \{ \frac { A _ { \mathbb { G } } + B _ { \mathbb { A } } + C _ { \mathbb { G } } } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 3 ( r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } , 2 } ^ { 2 } B _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } , 4 } ^ { 2 } C _ { \mathbb { A } } ) } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 5 } } \} } \\ & { \quad \quad \ + \frac { G ( M + m _ { \mathbb { G } } ) } { 2 m _ { \mathbb { G } } } \mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { G } } \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \{ \frac { A _ { \mathbb { G } } + B _ { \mathbb { G } } + C _ { \mathbb { G } } } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 ( r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } , 2 } ^ { 2 } B _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } , 4 } ^ { 2 } C _ { \mathbb { G } } ) } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 5 } }  } \\ &  \quad  \quad + \frac { G m _ { \mathbb { G } } } { 2 m _ { \mathbb { G } } } \mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { G } } \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \{ \frac { A _ { \mathbb { G } } + B _ { \mathbb { G } } + C _ { \mathbb { G } } } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 3 } } - \frac  3 ( r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } , 2 } ^ { 2 } B _ { \mathbb { G } } + r _ { \mathbb { G } , 5 } ^ { 2 } C _  \mathbb  G  \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \vec { r } _ { \odot } = - G ( M + m _ { \odot } ) \frac { r _ { \odot } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } - G m _ { \odot } [ \frac { r _ { \odot } - r _ { \textup { \bf q } } } { | r _ { \odot } - r _ { \textup { \bf q } } | ^ { 3 } } + \frac { r _ { \textup { \bf q } } } { r _ { \textup { \bf q } } ^ { 3 } } ] } \\ & { \quad \quad + \frac { G ( M + m _ { \odot } ) } { 2 M } { \bf T } _ { \diamond } \nabla _ { \odot } \{ \frac { A _ { \ S } + B _ { \delta } + C _ { \delta } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 ( r _ { \emptyset _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } A _ { \delta } + r _ { \odot _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } B _ { \bar { \ S } } + r _ { \odot _ { 3 } } ^ { 2 } C _ { \bar { \ S } } ) } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } }  } \\ & { \quad \quad  - \frac { 3 G m _ { \odot } } { 2 M r _ { \ Q } ^ { 5 } } { \bf T } _ { \diamond } \{ [ ( A _ { \delta } + B _ { \delta } + C _ { \delta } - \frac { 5 } { r _ { \mathrm { q } } ^ { 2 } } r _ { \textup { \bf q } } ^ { T } { \bf I } _ { \delta \pi } r _ { \textup { \bf q } } ) { \bf E } + 2 { \bf I } _ { \delta \pi } ] r _ { \textup { \bf q } } \}  } \\ & { \quad \quad  - \frac { 3 G m _ { \oplus } } { 2 m _ { \odot } r _ { \textup { \bf q } } ^ { 5 } } { \bf T } _ { \diamond } \{ [ ( A _ { \mathrm { q } } + B _ { \mathbb { G } } + C _ { \textup { \bf q } } \cdot \frac { 5 } { r _ { \mathrm { q } } ^ { 2 } } r _ { \textup { \bf q } } ^ { T } { \bf I } _ { \ Q \pi } r _ { \textup { \bf q } } ) { \bf E } + 2 { \bf I } _ { \mathbb { G } _ { F } } ] r _ { \textup { \bf q } } \} . } \end{array}
$$

The generalized equations of motion (3.118) for the geocentric motion of the Sun and Moon are well structured: If only the first line in each of the equations is taken into account, we obtain the equations of motion for the classical three-body problem with point masses. If only the first three lines of the equation for the Moon are taken into account, the equations for the generalized two-body problem Earth-Moon are obtained.

What still remains to be done is the derivation of the equations (3.88) for the rotation of Earth and Moon in the approximation used above for the orbital motion. As a matter of fact, we only have to deal with the right-hand sides of these equations,i.e., with the torques. In order to make our derivations as simple as possible, we compute the torques in the corresponding PAI-systems. Using the same development as in eqn. (3.104) we may write the first term in (3.88) as

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \int \rho _ { p } \nabla _ { \odot } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { | r _ { \odot } - r _ { p } | } \right\} \times \boldsymbol { r } _ { \odot } d V _ { \underline { { \delta } } } = } \\ & { \displaystyle \int _ { V _ { \ G } } \rho _ { p } \nabla _ { \odot } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { r _ { \odot } } + \frac { \boldsymbol { r } _ { \odot } \cdot \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { r _ { p } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( \boldsymbol { r } _ { \odot } \cdot \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } ) ^ { 2 } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } \right\} \times \boldsymbol { r } _ { \odot } d V _ { \underline { { \delta } } } . } \end{array}
$$

What at first sight looks like a formidable task becomes rather simple. because V(rn) × r = O. Therefore, we only have to consider the terms which result from taking the gradient of the numerators in the brackets $\{ \ldots \}$ above. This in turn immediately implies that only the contributions due to the second and the fourth term have to be considered. because the other two do not depend on ro. Taking into account that the second term is linear in the components of rp, the integral related to this term will be zero (center of mass condition). Therefore, only the contribution due to the last term, considering only the dependence $\mathrm { o n } \ r _ { \odot }$ of the numerator, will be different from zero:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \int _ { \psi _ { 6 } } \nabla _ { \odot } \{ \frac { 1 } { r _ { \odot } } + \frac { { r _ { 0 } } \cdot { r _ { p } } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { r _ { p } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { ( { r _ { \odot } } \cdot { r _ { p } } ) ^ { 2 } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } \} \times { r _ { \odot } } d V _ { \delta } } \\ & { \displaystyle = \int _ { { b } } \frac { 3 \rho _ { p } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } \nabla _ { \odot } \{ ( { r _ { \odot } ^ { \odot } } \cdot { r _ { p } } ) ^ { 2 } \} \times { { \dot { r } } _ { \odot } } d V _ { \delta } = \int _ { { b } } ^ { \frac { 3 \rho _ { p } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } } ( { r _ { \odot } } \cdot { r _ { p } } ) { r _ { p } } \times { r _ { \odot } } d V _ { \delta } } \\ & { \displaystyle = \int _ { { b } } \frac { 3 \rho _ { p } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } ( { r _ { \odot } } \cdot { r _ { p } } ) ( \frac { { { r _ { p } } _ { 2 } } { r _ { \odot } } _ { 3 } - { r _ { p } } { r _ { \odot } } _ { 2 } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } ) d V _ { \delta } = \frac { 3 } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } ( \begin{array} { l } { ( { C _ { \odot } } \cdot { R _ { \odot } } ) { r _ { \odot } } { r _ { \odot } } _ { 3 } } \\ { ( { A _ { \delta } } - { C _ { \delta } } ) { r _ { \odot } } { r _ { \odot } } _ { 2 } } \\ { { P _ { _ { 5 } } } { r _ { \odot } } _ { 2 } - { r _ { p } } { r _ { \odot } } _ { 1 } } \end{array} ) } \\ &  \displaystyle = \int _ { { b } } \frac { 3 \rho _ { p } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } ( { r _ { \odot } } \cdot { r _ { p } } ) ( \begin{array} { l } { { r _ { \odot } } { r _ { \odot } } _ { 3 } } \\  { r _ { \odot } }  r _   \end{array} \end{array}
$$

In the last step we made use of the fact that only the diagonal terms of the inertia tensor are different from zero in the PAI-system. For the computation of the diagonal terms the formulae (3.80) were used.

The result (3.120) may be transcribed to the Sun- and Earth-induced torque on the Moon in eqns. (3.88):

$$
\int _ { V _ { \mathrm { G } } } \rho _ { \varphi } \nabla _ { \mathrm { G } } \cdot \left\{ \frac { 1 } { \left| r _ { \mathrm { { \odot } } } - r _ { \mathrm { G } } - r _ { \mathrm { G } \mathrm { \odot } } \right| } \right\} \times r _ { \mathrm { c } } \ d V _ { \mathrm { G } } = \frac { 3 } { r _ { \mathrm { \odot } \mathrm { G } } ^ { 5 } } \left( \begin{array} { l } { \left( C _ { \mathrm { G } } - B _ { \mathrm { G } } \right) r _ { \mathrm { \odot } \mathrm { } \odot _ { \mathrm { 2 } } } r _ { \mathrm { \odot } \mathrm { \odot } _ { \mathrm { 3 } } } } \\ { \left( A _ { \mathrm { G } } - C _ { \mathrm { G } } \right) r _ { \mathrm { \odot } \mathrm { } \odot _ { \mathrm { 3 } } } r _ { \mathrm { \odot } \mathrm { 4 } } } \\ { \left( B _ { \mathrm { G } } - A _ { \mathrm { G } } \right) r _ { \mathrm { \odot } \mathrm { } \odot _ { \mathrm { 4 } } } r _ { \mathrm { \odot } \mathrm { 0 } _ { \mathrm { 2 } } } } \end{array} \right) \ .
$$

Note that the result has this form only if the Moon's PAI-system is used.

The computation of the torque exerted by the Earth on the Moon folows the same pattern as above. It is interesting to note that to the level of approximation of this section the result is the same as if the attracting mass were a point mass (and not an extended body). The result is given by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \int \int \rho _ { p } \rho _ { v } \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { \left| r _ { p } - r _ { \mathbb { G } } - r _ { \mathbb { G } } \right| } \right\} \times ( r _ { \mathbb { G } \rho } + r _ { \mathbb { G } } ) \ d V _ { \mathbb { G } } \ d V _ { \mathbb { G } } } \\ & { = \frac { 3 } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 5 } } \left( \frac { ( C _ { 5 } - B _ { \delta } ) r _ { \mathbb { G } } _ { 2 } r _ { \mathbb { G } } } { ( A _ { \delta } - C _ { \delta } ) r _ { \mathbb { G } } _ { 3 } r _ { \mathbb { G } } } \right) } \\ & { \displaystyle \int _ { V _ { \mathbb { G } } V _ { \mathbb { G } } } \rho _ { \rho _ { v } } \nabla _ { \mathbb { G } } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { \left| r _ { p } - r _ { \mathbb { G } } - r _ { \mathbb { G } } \right| } \right\} \times ( r _ { p } ^ { \star } - r _ { \mathbb { G } } ) \ d V _ { \mathbb { G } } \ d V _ { \mathbb { G } } } \\ & { = \frac { 3 M } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 5 } } \left( \frac { ( C _ { \mathbb { G } } - B _ { \epsilon } ) r _ { \mathbb { G } } r _ { \mathbb { G } } } { ( A _ { \mathbb { G } } - C _ { \mathbb { G } } ) r _ { \mathbb { G } } r _ { \mathbb { G } } } \right) . } \end{array}
$$

With the notation

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \gamma _ { \delta _ { 1 } } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \displaystyle \frac { C _ { \delta } - B _ { \delta } } { A _ { \delta } } ~ ; } } & { { \gamma _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 1 } } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \displaystyle \frac { C _ { \mathbb { Q } } - B _ { \mathbb { Q } } } { A _ { \mathbb { Q } } } } } \\ { { \gamma _ { \delta _ { 2 } } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \displaystyle \frac { A _ { \delta } - C _ { \delta } } { B _ { \delta } } ~ ; } } & { { \gamma _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 2 } } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \displaystyle \frac { A _ { \mathbb { Q } } - C _ { \mathbb { Q } } } { B _ { \mathbb { Q } } } } } \\ { { \gamma _ { \delta _ { 3 } } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \displaystyle \frac { B _ { \delta } - A _ { \delta } } { C _ { \delta } } ~ ; } } & { { \gamma _ { \mathbb { Q } _ { 3 } } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \displaystyle \frac { B _ { \mathbb { Q } } - A _ { \mathbb { Q } } } { C _ { \mathbb { Q } } } } } \end{array}
$$

the equations for the rotation of Earth and Moon may be written in the resp. PAI-systems as

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \left( \dot { \widetilde { \omega } } _ { 5 _ { 1 } } \right) _ { 4 } } & { + \left( \gamma _ { 5 _ { 1 } } \omega _ { 5 _ { 2 } } \omega _ { 5 _ { 1 } } \right) _ { 6 } } \\ { \left( \dot { \widetilde { \omega } } _ { 6 _ { 2 } } \right) _ { + } \left( \frac { \gamma _ { 6 _ { 1 } } \omega _ { 5 _ { 3 } } \omega _ { 7 _ { 1 } } } { \gamma _ { 5 _ { 3 } } \omega _ { 5 _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 5 _ { 2 } } } \right) } & { = + \frac { 3 G m _ { G } } { r _ { 9 } ^ { 5 } } \left( \gamma _ { 5 _ { 1 } } \gamma _ { 7 _ { 2 } } \gamma _ { 6 _ { 1 } } \gamma _ { 7 _ { 2 } } \right) } \\ & { + \frac { 3 G m _ { G } } { r _ { 9 } ^ { 7 } } \left( \gamma _ { 9 _ { 1 } } r _ { 1 0 } \gamma _ { 7 0 } \gamma _ { 8 _ { 1 } } \right) } \\ & { + \frac { 3 G m _ { G } } { r _ { 9 } ^ { 9 } } \left( \gamma _ { 9 _ { 2 } } r _ { 9 _ { 2 } } \gamma _ { 9 _ { 1 } } \right) } \\ { \left( \dot { \widetilde { \omega } } _ { 8 } r _ { 1 } \right) _ { 1 } } & { + \left( \gamma _ { 1 0 } \omega _ { 2 } \omega _ { 5 } \omega _ { 1 0 } \right) _ { 1 } } \\ { \left( \dot { \widetilde { \omega } } _ { 9 _ { 4 _ { 2 } } } \right) _ { + } \left( \gamma _ { 7 6 _ { 2 } } \omega _ { 5 _ { 3 } } \omega _ { 1 0 } \right) _ { 0 } } & { + \frac { 3 G M } { r _ { 9 } ^ { 8 } } \left( \gamma _ { 9 _ { 1 } } r _ { 7 6 _ { 2 } } \gamma _ { 7 6 _ { 3 } } \right) } \\ & { + \frac { 3 G m _ { G } } { r _ { 9 } ^ { 9 } } \left( \gamma _ { 7 6 _ { 1 } } r _ { 1 0 } \gamma _ { 7 6 _ { 1 } } \right) } \end{array}
$$

Obviously the equations show a particularly simple structure. For the solution of the equations in the inertial system we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \dot { h } _ { 6 } = + \frac { 3 G m _ { G } } { r _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { T } _ { 5 } \left( \frac { ( C _ { b } - B _ { \xi } ) r _ { G _ { 2 } } r _ { G _ { 3 } } } { ( B _ { G } - C _ { \xi } ) r _ { G _ { 3 } } r _ { G _ { 1 } } } \right) } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { 3 G m _ { G } } { r _ { \phi _ { 0 } } ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { T } _ { 5 } \left( \frac { ( C _ { b } - B _ { \xi } ) r _ { G _ { 2 } } r _ { G _ { 3 } } } { ( B _ { G } - C _ { \xi } ) r _ { G _ { 3 } } r _ { G _ { 1 } } } \right) } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { 3 G m _ { G } } { r _ { \phi _ { 0 } } ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { T } _ { 5 } \left( \frac { ( C _ { b } - B _ { \xi } ) r _ { \phi _ { 2 } } r _ { \phi _ { 3 } } } { ( B _ { G } - A _ { \xi } ) r _ { G _ { 0 } } r _ { G _ { 2 } } } \right) } \\ & { \qquad \dot { h } _ { \ G } = + \frac { 3 G M } { r _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { T } _ { \ G } \left( \frac { ( C _ { q } - B _ { \xi } ) r _ { G _ { 2 } } r _ { G _ { 1 } } } { ( A _ { G } - C _ { \xi } ) r _ { G _ { 3 } } r _ { G _ { 1 } } } \right) } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { 3 G m _ { G } } { r _ { \phi _ { 0 } } ^ { 2 } } \mathbf { T } _ { \ G } \left( \frac { ( C _ { q } - B _ { \xi } ) r _ { G _ { 2 } } r _ { G _ { 3 } } } { ( A _ { G } - C _ { G } ) r _ { G _ { 0 } } r _ { G _ { 0 } } } \right) } \end{array}
$$

As usual the terms $\mathbf { T } _ { \delta } \left( . . . \right)$ have to be evaluated in the Earth's PAI-system, the terms $\mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { ( , ~ } \cdot \cdot \mathcal { ) } }$ in the Moon's PAI-system.

In the above approximation the generalized three-body problem Earth-Sun-Moon is described by the equations (3.118) and either eqns. (3.124. 3.68) if the rotational motion is described in the PAI-systens or eqns. (3.125. 3.66), if this motion is described in the inertial system. These systems of equations represent the most general three-body problem with rigid bodies considered here.

3.3.6 Second Order Differential Equations for the Euler Angles $\psi$ ， $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ and $\Theta$

The rotational motion of the Earth and the Moon are defined by the equations (3.88) and the corresponding kinematic Euler equations (3.68) (one set of kinematic equations must be used for Earth rotation, one for the rotation of the Moon). Together, the equations (3.88) and (3.68) form one set of $2 \cdot 6$ first-order differential equations.

When solving the generalized three-body problem, i.e., when solving simulta-neously the equations for the orbital and rotational motion of Earth, Moon, and Sun, it would be preferable to transform the first order differential equa-tion system for the Euler angles and the components of the angular velocity vector into one second order system for the three Euler angles. This can be achieved easily by taking the time derivative of Euler's kinematic equations (3.68):

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( \begin{array} { c } { \ddot { \psi } _ { \dot { \varepsilon } } } \\ { \ddot { \varepsilon } _ { \dot { \varepsilon } } } \\ { \ddot { \theta } _ { \dot { \varepsilon } } } \end{array} ) = ( \begin{array} { c c } { - \sin \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } \csc \varepsilon _ { \dot { \delta } } - \cos \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } \csc \varepsilon _ { \dot { \delta } } 0 } \\ { - \cos \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } } & { + \sin \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } } \\ { \sin \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } \cot \varepsilon _ { \dot { \delta } } + \cos \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } \cot \varepsilon _ { \dot { \delta } } 1 } \end{array} ) \dot { \omega } _ { \dot { \varepsilon } \mathcal { F } } ( t ) } \\ { +  \frac { d } { d t } ( \begin{array} { c c } { - \sin \theta _ { \dot { \varepsilon } } \csc \varepsilon _ { \dot { \delta } } - \cos \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } \csc \varepsilon _ { \dot { \delta } } 0 } \\ { - \cos \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } } & { + \sin \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } } \\ { \sin \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } \cot \varepsilon _ { \dot { \delta } } + \cos \theta _ { \dot { \delta } } \cot \varepsilon _ { \dot { \delta } } 1 } \end{array} ) \omega _ { \dot { \delta } \mathcal { F } } ( t ) \ . } \end{array}
$$

Using the differential equations (3.88) for the components of the angular velocity vectors and Euler's kinematic equations in the form (3.67) on the right-hand sides of the above equations, one easily obtains a second-order system for the Euler angles:

$$
\left( \begin{array} { c } { \ddot { \psi } _ { \delta } } \\ { \ddot { \varepsilon } _ { \delta } } \\ { \ddot { \theta } _ { \delta } } \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array} { c } { \left( - \sin \theta _ { \delta } \dot { \omega } _ { \delta } _ { \delta } - \cos \theta _ { \delta } \dot { \omega } _ { \delta } _ { \delta } + \dot { \varepsilon } _ { \delta } \dot { \theta } _ { \delta } - \dot { \varepsilon } _ { \delta } \dot { \psi } _ { \delta } \right) / \sin \varepsilon _ { \delta } } \\ { - \cos \theta _ { \delta } \dot { \omega } _ { \delta } _ { \sigma _ { \updownarrow } } + \sin \theta _ { \delta } \dot { \omega } _ { \delta } _ { \delta , \mathbf { \rho } } - \dot { \psi } _ { \delta } \dot { \theta } _ { \delta } \sin \varepsilon _ { \delta } } \\ { - \ddot { \psi } _ { \delta } \cos \varepsilon _ { \delta } + \dot { \varepsilon } _ { \delta } \dot { \psi } _ { \delta } \sin \varepsilon _ { \delta } + \dot { \omega } _ { \delta , \mathbf { \rho } } } \end{array} \right) \mathrm { ~ . ~ }
$$

Equations (3.127) actually are second-order differential equations in the Euler angles, because the first derivatives of the components of the angular velocity vector $\omega _ { \delta }$ in the Earth-fixed PAI-system only contain the components of this vector (which may in turn be written as functions of the Euler angles thanks to the kinematic equations (3.67)) and the components of the perturbing bodies in the Earth-fixed PAI-system, which are obtained by the corresponding components in the Earth-fixed PAI-system and the Euler angles as transformation parameters.

By replacing the subscript “ $\dot { \delta } ^ { \dag }$ by the subscript $^ { 6 6 } \textcircled { \langle } ^ { 7 7 }$ in the above equations, one obtains the corresponding relations for the Moon.

When using the equations (3.127) instead of the first-order version (3.88), (3.68)one has to use the kinematic equations in the form (3.67) whenever the angular velocity vector is required.

For analytical investigations the first-order version of the equations is usually given the preference, when numerically solving the equations. the version (3.127) is better suited.

# 3.3.7 Kinematics of the Non-Rigid Earth

Strictly speaking, expressions like body-fired coordinate systein, rotation and angular velocity of a celestial body become meaningless when departing from the rigid-body model and allowing for deformations.

It is intuitively clear what has to be understood by the deformation of a celestial body: As opposed to the -rigid body approximation one allows for the distances between individual mass elements of the body to vary in time. These variations are due to forces acting between the mass elements. The relevant deformations in the case of the Earth are small: the tidal deformations (mainly) due to Moon and Sun are, e.g., only of the order of a few decimeters on the Earth's surface and on the open ocean - an effect with a relative amplitude of about $1 0 ^ { - 7 }$ when measured in Earth radii.

The center of mass of a deformable body is defined in the same way as that of a rigid body: The definition (3.55) may be taken over without any changes - one only has to keep in mind that the physical shape of the volume V occupied by the body may change in time. The saine is true for the definition of a celestial body's inertia tensor: The definition (3.74) is suitable for a deformable body as well.

It is therefore still possible to write the position vector $x _ { p }$ (see Figure 3.2) of a particular volume element as the sum of the position vector of the Earth's center of mass $x _ { \delta }$ and the geocentric position vector $\boldsymbol { r } _ { \mathit { l } } )$ of the volume element :

$$
{ \pmb x } _ { p } = { \pmb x } _ { \updownarrow } + { \pmb r } _ { p } \ .
$$

In view of the fact that the defornations of an Earth-like planet are in general small, it makes sense to define a rigid, rotating coordinate system w.r.t. which the actual deformations of the body remain small at all times. Let us mention that such a system may not exist for all celestial bodies. In the case of the Sun there are, e.g., latitude dependent angular velocities of solar rotation, which would invalidate this concept.

Having introduced the rigid, rotating coordinate system, it is natural to associate the angular velocity vector $\omega _ { \delta }$ with the rotating coordinate system, implying in turn that the three Euler angles $\psi _ { \xi }$ ， $\varepsilon _ { \delta }$ and $\boldsymbol { \Theta } _ { \boldsymbol { \xi } }$ describe the orientation (attitude) of this rotating coordinate system in inertial space.

Equations (3.56) describe the transformation of the position vector of an arbitrary volume element of the rigid Earth between the inertial and the Earth-fixed PAI-systems. The same equations describe the transition betwcen the inertial and the rigid, rotating system in the case of deformable bodies.

For a rigid Earth the velocity of a volume element of the Earth may be written as the sum of the velocity of the Earth's center of mass and the geocentric velocity of the element, which in turn is defined by the vector product (3.58). If we allow for deformations we have to use the more general relation

$$
\dot { \boldsymbol { r } } _ { p } = \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \delta } \times \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } + \delta \dot { \boldsymbol { r } } _ { p } \mathrm { ~ , ~ }
$$

where $\delta \dot { r } _ { p }$ describes the motion of the volune element relative to the rotating system.

With the above interpretation of the Euler angles Euler's kinematic equations, i.e., the relations between the components of the angular velocity vector Wg and the angular velocities Ψg, εg, @t, also may be used for the case of the non-rigid Earth.

The definitions (3.74) and (3.7o) for the inertia tensor and the angular momentum may be used for non-rigid bodies as well. One has to keep in mind, however, that there is in general no coordinate system w.r.t. which all elements of the inertia tensor are constant in time.

The angular momentun of a deformable Earth is given by the defiition (3.70),where the representation (3.129)for the velocitiesof the Earth's volume elements has to be used:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle h _ { \delta } = \int _ { V _ { \delta } } \rho ( \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } ) ~ \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } \times \dot { \boldsymbol { r } } _ { p } ~ d V _ { \delta } = \int _ { V _ { \delta } } \rho ( \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } ) ~ \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } \times \{ \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \delta } \times \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } + \delta \dot { \boldsymbol { r } } _ { p } \} ~ d V _ { \delta } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { \displaystyle = { \bf I } _ { \mathcal { F } _ { e } } \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \delta } + \int _ { V _ { \delta } } \rho ( \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } ) ~ \boldsymbol { r } _ { p } \times \delta \dot { \boldsymbol { r } } _ { p } ~ d V _ { \delta } = { \bf I } _ { \delta _ { \mathcal { F } } } \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \delta } + \kappa _ { \delta } ~ , } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf { I } _ { \mathtt { \delta } _ { \mathcal { F } } }$ is the Earth's inertia tensor expressed in a rigid, rotating coordinate system and $\kappa _ { \delta } ( t )$ is the angular momentum of the deformable Earth relative to the same system. Note that eqns. (3.130) are the generalized relations (3.73) between the angular momentum $^ h$ of a deformable planet and the angular velocity vector $\boldsymbol { \omega }$ characterizing its rotation.

Up till now the definition of the rigid. rotating coordinate system was sonewhat arbitrary: We just asked for a system relative to which the deformations would be small if possible at all times. A particularly suitable rigid, rotating system may be defined by the requirement

$$
\kappa _ { \delta } ( t ) \stackrel { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } 0
$$

for all times $t$ . The idea of defining the rotating frame by asking the angular momentum due to deformations to vanish for all times is attributed to Félix Tissérand (1845 1896) (see [121]). The coordinate System F thus has a slightly different meaning in the case of the deformable Earth. It may no longer be defined as an Earth-fxed system, but as the system rotating with the rigid coordinate system. w.r.t. which there is no inner angular momentum.

Equations (3.131) are the condition equations for the realization of a Tisserand system. The actual realization of a Tissérand system is far from trivial, because we have no direct access to the velocities and densities in the Earth's interior. Usually, Tissérand systems are realized in a purely kineinatic way using the coordinates and velocities of the space geodetic observing sites on the Earth's crust.

Tissérand systems are particularly suitable, because the equations of motion are formally very similar to those of the rigid body when expressed in this system.

# 3.3.8 Liouville-Euler Equations of Earth Rotation

For the derivation of the (orbital and rotational) equations of motion in the case of deformable bodies we have to depart from the basic equations of motion (3.81) for individual volume elements of Earth (and Moon) as in the rigid-body case. These equations then have to be combined according to exactly the same pattern as in the case of the rigid body in order to obtain the equations of motion for the center of mass and for the angular momentum of the Earth.

When reviewing the derivation of the results (3.85) and (3.88) we observe, that we actually did not have to assume the rigidity of celestial bodies. We were only relying on the basic law actio=reactio between any two individual mass elements. As long as this assumption holds, the equations of motion (3.85), (3.88) and (3.90) remain the same for deformable bodies as for rigid bodies.

Naturally, one has to keep in mind that the angular momentum $h _ { \delta }$ of the planet is now defined by eqn. (3.130), giving rise to the following equations for Earth rotation:

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left\{ { \bf I } _ { \delta _ { \mathcal F } } \omega _ { \delta } + \kappa _ { \delta } \right\} = \ell _ { \updownarrow } + \ell _ { \odot \updownarrow } .
$$

Equations (3.132) are vector equations. They may, however, also be interpreted as equations in the coordinates of the inertial system. Note that eqn. (3.132), defining the rotation of a non-rigid body in inertial space, and the first of eqns. (3.9o), defining the same motion for a rigid body, are formally identical, if a Tissérand system is used in the former case. Observe also,however, that $\mathbf { I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } }$ cannot be transformed into a system, where all of its elements are time independent.

The torques $\ell _ { \odot ^ { \pm } }$ and $\ell _ { \mathbb { ( } ^ { } \textrm { \scriptsize { \textrm { E } } } }$ are defined by the integrals on the right-hand side of eqns. (3.88). When evaluating these integrals, one of course would have to take the deformations into account, as well. In view of the fact that the torques are smal quantities, the rigid-body approximation is in practice good enough for the computation of these integrals.

In section 3.3 it was argued that the equations for the rotation of the Earth are particularly simple when referred to the body-fixed PAI-system. We can-not expect a comparable gain, when transforming the equations for Earth rotation into the rigid, rotating coordinate system - just because the inertia tensor wil neither become diagonal nor time-invariant in this system.

For Earth-like planets it is, however, always possible to introduce a rigid, rotating coordinate system w.r.t. which the of-diagonal eleinents of the inertia tensor and the time-varying part of the diagonal elements are small when compared to the diagonal elements of the corresponding rigid body.

The transformation of eqns. (3.132) from the inertial into the rigid, rotating coordinate system follows the pattern of the transformation from the inertial into the Earth's PAI-system in the case of the rigid body (see section 3.3.4). After a few rather tedious, but elementary algebraic transformations one obtains:

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left\{ { \bf I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } } \omega _ { \delta } + \kappa _ { \delta } \right\} + \omega _ { \delta } \times \Big \{ { \bf I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } } \omega _ { \delta } + \kappa _ { \delta } \Big \} = \ell _ { \left( \delta \right. } + \ell _ { \left. \odot \right) } ,
$$

where:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \ell _ { \mathbb { G } \delta } = \frac { G m _ { \mathbb { G } } } { r _ { \mathbb { G } } ^ { 5 } } r _ { \mathbb { G } } \times ( \mathbf { I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } } r _ { \mathbb { G } } ) } \\ & { \ell _ { \mathbb { G } \delta } = \frac { G m _ { \odot } } { r _ { \odot } ^ { 5 } } r _ { \odot } \times ( \mathbf { I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } } r _ { \odot } ) \ . } \end{array}
$$

When using a Tisserand system, equations (3.133) assume the following particularly simple form

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left\{ { \bf I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } } \omega _ { \delta } \right\} + \omega _ { \delta } \times \left\{ { \bf I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } } \omega _ { \delta } \right\} = \ell _ { \mathbb { C } \delta } + \ell _ { \odot \delta } .
$$

Equations (3.135) are usually referred to as the Liouville-Euler equations of Earth rotation (named after Joseph Liouville (1809-1882) and Euler).

Note that for the special case of the rigid body eqns. (3.135) reduce to the simpler equations (3.124)， if the PAI-system is used. For a rigid body we might use the Liouville-Euler equations (3.135) as the equations of motion referring to an arbitrary body-fixed system. In this case we might even make use of the fact that the inertia tensor is not a function of time and write

$$
\mathbf { I } _ { \delta _ { \mathcal { F } } } \dot { \pmb { \omega } } _ { \delta } + \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \delta } \times \left\{ \mathbf { I } _ { \delta _ { \mathcal { F } } } \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \delta } \right\} = \ell _ { \mathbb { C } \delta } + \ell _ { \odot \delta } \mathrm { ~ , ~ }
$$

whereas we have to take into account the time variability of the inertia tensor for non-rigid Earth models:

$$
\mathbf { i } _ { \mathcal { F } e } \omega _ { \mathcal { F } e } + \mathbf { I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } } \dot { \omega } _ { \delta } + \omega _ { \delta } \times \left\{ \mathbf { I } _ { \delta \mathcal { F } } \omega _ { \delta } \right\} = \ell _ { \mathbb { C } \delta } + \ell _ { \odot \delta } .
$$

We are now in a position to solve the Liouvile-Euler equations, provided the inertia tensor and its time derivative are known. For this purpose we have to specify the nature of the deformations considered. This discussion will be a central issue in Chapter II- 2.

# 3.4 Equations of Motion for an Artificial Earth Satellite

# 3.4.1 Introduction

The equations of motion for artificial Earth satellites and their derivation from the Newtonian axioms is closely related to the developments related to the Earth-Moon-Sun system. Figure 3.3 may also serve to describe the imotion of an artificial Earth-satelite in its orbit around our planet. It reminds us, that in general, artificial Earth satellites should be viewed as “extended" objects. A complete description of the satelite's motion comprises the motion of its center of mass and the orientation of a satellite-fixed coordinate system (with origin in the center of mass of the satellite) in inertial space. In the context of artificial satellites this orientation w.r.t. inertial space is called the attitude of the satellite. Figure 3.5 illustrates the body-fixed coordinate system for a satellite of the US Global Positioning System.

![](images/197567582cb71e15d105d9ac343f0d30cd17c90248da6d63985abae4519c1af4.jpg)  
Fig. 3.5. Body-fxed coordinate system of a GPS satellite

Whereas the orientation of Earth and Moon is defined uniquely by Euler's equations (3.124), the same cannot be true for active satellites, like e.g., GPS satellites. These navigation satellites have to orient their antennas (along the positive $z$ -axis) always (more or less) towards the Earth's center, in order to optimize signal reception for navigation and positioning on the Earth's surface or in the Earth-near space (precise orbit determination for LEOs). The solar panels of the GPS satelite provide the energy for the operation of the satelites. In order to optimize the energy gain, the panels’ surfaces have to be perpendicular to the direction Sun-satellite at all times. This is why the attitude of the satellite has to be actively controlled. For GPS satellites the nominal attitude is maintained with momentum wheels, nounted on the axes of the satellites. Only occasionally, when the momentum wheels in the satellites are spinning too rapidly, the rotation of the wheels has to be stopped, and the attitude has to be maintained using thrusters (this de-spinning of the wheels is called “momentum dump"). If the attitude of satellites is maintained actively,the Euler equations for the rotation of a body are completely ruled out (or they should be modified to include the torques applied by these mechanisms).

There are satellites, for which the orientation is not important. The satellites Lageos I and Lageos II (Lageos II is shown in Figure 2.4 in Chapter 2) were, e.g., designed as massive, spherically symmetric bodies with Laser retro-refectors distributed over the satellites’ surface. As the name implies, the satellites were constructed to be observed by the Laser observation technique (for a precise definition of the Laser measurement see Chapter I1-3). The reduction of the distance measurement from the actual reflector to the satellite's center of mass is trivial and needs no knowledge of the attitude of the (spherically symmetric) satellite. Also, the mass distribution within the satellite is almost perfectly spherically symmetric, which is why the attitude of the satellite should not have any sizeable influence on the motion of the center of mass.

The two examples indicate that in satellite geodesy often the attitude plays a lesser role than the orbital motion, or, if the attitude is important, it is established by active control mechanisms onboard the satellite, momentum wheels and thrusters being the important tools for attitude maintenance. However, as soon as a satellite is not (or is no longer) actively controlled, its attitude may be derived from Euler's equations describing the rotation of a rigid body. This is why in section 3.4.3 we include the equations governing a rigid satellite's attitude under the assumptions that there are no active control mechanisms onboard. In the same section we also discuss the validity of separation of orbital and rotational motion for artificial satellites. In section 3.4.2 we uniquely focus uniquely on the motion of a satellite's center of mass.

# 3.4.2 Equations for the Center of Mass of a Satellite

Apart from the atitude-related issues mentioned in the introductory section 3.4.1 the following differences w.r.t. the equations of motion dealt with so far are relevant:

The mass of an artificial satellite always may be neglected w.r.t to the masses of Earth, Moon, Sun, and planets. This aspect considerably reduces the complexity of the problein because we do not have to worry about the accelerations exerted by the satellite on these celestial bodies. The orbits and orientation of Earth, Sun and Moon, and planets, required to compute the satellite's motion, may be assumed as known. · Due to the proximity of artificial Earth satellites with height above surface ranging from 150 - 200 km (below this height above the Earth's surface an orbiting object will decay rather rapidly) up to, let us say, the geosta-tionary belt at a geostationary distance of about 42ooo km, it is no longer sufficient to take only the main term and the second-order terms of the Earth's gravitational potential into account. A much more complete de-scription including hundreds of terms of the stationary part of the Earth's gravitational potential is needed.

· The tidal deformations of the Earth (solid Earth and ocean tides) have to be modeled as well when computing the gravitational attraction acting on the satellite.

· Non-gravitational forces like

- Air drag due to the Earth's upper atmosphere, solar radiation pressure effects,   
thruster firings,   
$^ -$ etc.   
have to be considered as well.

The first of the above aspects is the only one reducing the complexity of the problem. It allows us to address the equations for one satellite at the time, which is why usually only three differential equations (of second order) for the center of mass (and possibly three more equations describing the attitude) are considered subsequently. The second aspect does not pose delicate problems, in particular if the point mass approximation is used. The third aspect, the generalization of the Earth's gravitational potential, is the main topic of this section. Non-gravitational forces (aspect 5) will be dealt with in Chapter II- 3 together with the forces associated with the aspects of a non-rigid Earth. The discussion of tidal deformations is postponed to Chapter II- 3.

The Equations of Motion in the Inertial System. In the inertial system the equations of motion for an artificial satellite follow directly from the Newtonian axioms. In analogy to eqns. (3.81) the equations of motion of the center of mass $_ { x }$ of a satellite of mass $m$ may be set up as follows:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { m \displaystyle { \ddot { \pmb x } } = - G m \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } \displaystyle \frac { \pmb x - \pmb x _ { p } } { | \pmb x - \pmb x _ { p } | ^ { 3 } } ~ d V _ { \delta } } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { \qquad - G m \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \displaystyle \frac { \pmb x - \pmb x _ { j } } { | \pmb x - \pmb x _ { j } | ^ { 3 } } ~ + ~ \sum _ { j _ { \mathrm { n g } } } \pmb \rho _ { \mathrm { n g } } ~ . } } \end{array}
$$

where $\sum f _ { \mathrm { n g } }$ is the sum of non-gravitational forces acting on the satellite. Apart from the Earth's gravitational attraction the gravitational effects of $n \geq 2$ celestial bodies have to be modelled (Sun and Moon and possibly (other) planets)， where the point mass model can be adopted for all perturbing bodies. This is justified in most cases due to the distances of the satellite w.r.t. these bodies. For very ambitious applications (very long satellite arcs) the term associated with lunar gravitation would have to be taken into account by a volume integral, as well.

Dividing all terms of the above equation by the mass of the satelite gives the aceleration of the satellite in the inertial reference frane. Using moreover the relative density function, we obtain the equation of motion of the satelite in the inertial system, with $M = { \mathrm { m a s s } }$ of the Earth:

$$
\ddot { x } = - G M \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } \frac { x - x _ { p } } { | x - x _ { p } | ^ { 3 } } d V _ { \delta } - G \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \frac { x - x _ { j } } { | x - x _ { j } | ^ { 3 } } + \sum a _ { \mathrm { n g } } + \ . . . \ .
$$

where $a _ { \mathrm { n g } }$ are the non-gravitational accelerations (better: forces per mass unit) acting on the satellite. In the same approximation (i.e., point mass approximation to describe the relative motion of all celestial bodies except that of the satellite) the equations of motion for the Earth's center of mass may be written as (compare eqns. (3.82)):

$$
\ddot { \pmb { x } } _ { \pmb { \updownarrow } } = - G \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \frac { { \pmb { x } } _ { \updownarrow } - { \pmb { x } } _ { j } } { | { \pmb { x } } _ { \updownarrow } - { \pmb { x } } _ { j } | ^ { 3 } } .
$$

For highest accuracies one might use the equation (3.82) to model the motion of the Earth's center of mass. For all applications we consider in this book, the approximation (3.140) is suffcient. The generalization is straight forward and may be left to the reader.

The Equations of Motion in the Geocentric System. Subtracting eqn. (3.140) from eqn. (3.139) leads to the equation for the geocentric motion of the satellite' center of mass, $\pmb { r } \overset { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } x - \pmb { x } _ { \delta }$ (the geocentric position vectors for the other bodies are defined in the same way).

$$
\begin{array} { l }  { \displaystyle { \ddot { r } = - G M \int _ { V _ { b } } \rho _ { p _ { r } } { \frac { r - r _ { p } } { | r - r _ { p } | ^ { 3 } } d V _ { \delta } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ - G \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \left\{ { \frac { r - r _ { j } } { | r - r _ { j } | ^ { 3 } } + \frac { r _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } } \right\} ~ + ~ \sum a _ { \mathrm { n g } } ~ + ~ . . . ~ . } } \end{array}
$$

The above equation is a vector equation. It may, however, also interpreted as an equation in Cartesian coordinates. Due to the fact that the equatorial plane is in an excellent approximation a plane of symmetry of the Earth, it makes sense to refer the above equations to the equatorial geocentric coordinate system of a particular reference epoch (which we always select as the system $J 2 0 0 0 . 0$ ). The system is a so-called quasi-inertial system (as it is not rotating w.r.t. any inertial system, but its origin is attached to the Earth's center of mass).

The gravitational terms on the right-hand sides of these equations may be written as gradients of a potential function.

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \ddot { r } = G M \nabla \int _ { V _ { \Phi } } \frac { \rho _ { p _ { r } } } { | r - r _ { p } | } d V _ { \delta } } } \\ { \displaystyle { + G \nabla \left[ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { | r - r _ { j } | } + \frac { r _ { j } \cdot r } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } \right\} \right] + \sum a _ { \mathrm { n g } } + . . . ~ . } } \end{array}
$$

Note that tidal effects. which are due to the Earth's deformation caused by the gravitational attraction of Moon and Sun (see Chapter I- 2). are included in the equations of motion (3.142) provided the volume occupied by the Earth's body and the density $\rho _ { p _ { r } }$ are considered as time-varying quantities. In order to simplify the discussion, we will assume for the remainder of this chapter that the Earth is rigid and postpone the discussion of the tidal potential to Chapters II- 2 and II-3.

It was already mentioned that the equations of motion (3.142) may also be interpreted as equations in the components of vector $\mathbf { \gamma } ^ { * }$ in the geocentric quasi-inertial system. It is, however, much more convenient to evaluate the volume integral in the Earth's PAI-system and to evaluate the gradient in this system. This is achieved by transforming the component matrix from the inertial to the Earth's PAI-system. taking the gradient in this system system， and then by transforming the gravitational acceleration due to the Earth into the inertial system. Using this concept. the equations of motion in the geocentric quasi-inertial system may be written in the form

$$
\ddot { \boldsymbol { r } } = G M \mathbf { T } _ { \delta } \nabla V ( \boldsymbol { r } ) + G \nabla \left[ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { j } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { | \boldsymbol { r } - \boldsymbol { r } _ { j } | } + \frac { \boldsymbol { r } _ { j } \cdot \boldsymbol { r } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } \right\} \right] + \sum a _ { \mathrm { n g } } + \dots .
$$

with the understanding that all component matrices in the equations of motion (3.143) refer to the inertial system, except for the term VV(r), which refers to the Earth's PAI-system. The back-transformation into the inertial system is performed by the matrix $\mathbf { r } _ { \textrm { \tiny \frac { 1 } { 3 } } }$ , eqn. (3.56), which is why $\mathbf { T } _ { \mathbb { S } } ~ \nabla V ( r )$ is a component matrix referring to the quasi-inertial geocentric system (as all other terms on the right-hand side of eqn. (3.143)).

The Earth's Stationary Gravitational Potential. According to eqn. (3.143) the potential function of the Earth may be writen as

$$
\begin{array}{c} V ( r ) = G M \int _ { \stackrel { { \scriptstyle \vert r - \ r _ { p } \vert } } { { \scriptstyle \vert r _ { \hat { \delta } } \end{array} } \vert } } d V _ { \hat { \delta } } \ .
$$

In this definition $V ( r )$ is always positive. One easily verifies that the following equation holds by taking first the gradient $\nabla V \left( r \right)$ of the above scalar function. then the divergence $\nabla \cdot \nabla V ( \boldsymbol { r } ) \stackrel { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \Delta V ( \boldsymbol { r } )$ of the gradient:

$$
\Delta V ( \pmb { r } ) \stackrel { \mathrm { d e f } } { = } \bigg \{ \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial r _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \bigg \} V ( \pmb { r } ) = 0 \ ,
$$

where $r _ { i } , \ i = 1 , 2 , 3$ , are the Cartesian coordinates of the vector $\tau ^ { * }$ in the Earth-fixed system (the equation would of course hold in any Cartesian coordinate system). Equation (3.145) is called the Laplace equation. This equation holds for any potential for vectors $\tau$ outside the mass distribution (for satellites, as long as they are in orbit $( ! )$ , this condition is certainly met).

As the mass distribution within the Earth is close to spherically symmetric, it makes sense to express Laplace's equation in spherical, rather than rectangular coordinates. Let us introduce the following spherical coordinates in the Earth-fixed system:

$$
{ \begin{array} { l l l } { { r _ { 1 } = r \cos \phi \cos \lambda ; } } & { { r = { \sqrt { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + r _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } } } \\ { { r _ { 2 } = r \cos \phi \sin \lambda ; } } & { { \phi = \arcsin { \frac { r _ { 3 } } { \sqrt { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + r _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } } } } \\ { { r _ { 3 } = r \sin \phi ; } } & { { \lambda = \arctan { \frac { r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } } } , } } \end{array} }
$$

where r is the geocentric distance. Φ the latitude. and X the longitude of the satellite. Φ and X are geocentric coordinates, where the longitude X is positive East of the Greenwich meridian. For points on the northern hemisphere the Iatitude Φ is positive, for the southern hemisphere it is negative.

It is a tedious, but straightforward exercise to transform the Laplace equation from rectangular to spherical coordinates:

$$
\left\{ { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { \partial } { \partial r } } \left( r ^ { 2 } { \frac { \partial } { \partial r } } \right) \ + \ { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \cos \phi } } { \frac { \partial } { \partial \phi } } \left( \cos \phi { \frac { \partial } { \partial \phi } } \right) \ + \ { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } } { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \lambda ^ { 2 } } } \right\} V ( r ) = 0
$$

where

$$
\varDelta = \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial } { \partial r } \left( r ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial } { \partial r } \right) + \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \cos \phi } \frac { \partial } { \partial \phi } \left( \cos \phi \frac { \partial } { \partial \phi } \right) + \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \lambda ^ { 2 } }
$$

is the Laplace operator in spherical coordinates.

Every solution of the Laplace equation, be it expressed in rectangular (3.145) or in spherical coordinates (3.147),is called a harmonic function. Solutions of eqn. (3.147) are called spherical harmonic functions, or briefly spherical harmonics.

It is particularly attractive that the solutions of the second order partial differential equation (3.147) may be separated in spherical coordinates:

$$
V ( \boldsymbol { r } ) = R ( \boldsymbol { r } ) \varPhi ( \phi ) \varLambda ( \lambda ) \ .
$$

A formal proof for this equation and the derivation of its solution may be found in any treatment of potential theory and in many textbooks of physical geodesy. Let us mention in particular the very concise treatment by Kaula [62] and the more elaborate and complete treatments in [52] and [124]. The final result may be brought into the following form:

$$
V ( r , \lambda , \phi ) = { \frac { G M } { r } } \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \left( { \frac { a _ { \hat { \varsigma } } } { r } } \right) ^ { i } \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { i } P _ { i } ^ { k } ( \sin \phi ) \big \{ C _ { i k } \cos k \lambda + S _ { i k } \sin k \lambda \big \} \ ,
$$

where $a _ { \delta } \approx 6 3 7 8 1 3 7$ m (see Table I- 2.1) is the equatorial radius of the Earth. The functions $P _ { i } ^ { k } ( x )$ are the associated Legendre functions, which may be defined as follows:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { P _ { i } ^ { 0 } ( x ) = \displaystyle { \tilde { P _ { i } } ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { i } i ! } \frac { d ^ { i } } { d x ^ { i } } \Big \{ ( x ^ { 2 } - 1 ) ^ { i } \Big \} } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { P _ { i } ^ { k } ( x ) = ( 1 - x ^ { 2 } ) ^ { \frac { k } { 2 } } \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { k } } { d x ^ { k } } \big \{ P _ { i } ( x ) \big \} } ~ , \quad k = 0 , 1 , \ldots , i ~ , } }  \end{array}
$$

where $P _ { i } ( x )$ are the Legendre polynomials as they were already introduced in eqns. (3.101). The coefficients $C _ { i k }$ and $S _ { i k }$ are defined as (see, e.g.,[124]):

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C _ { i 0 } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { a _ { \delta } ^ { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } } \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r ^ { i } P _ { i } ( \sin \phi _ { p } ) ~ d V ~ ; ~ i \ge 0 } } \\ { { \displaystyle C _ { i k } = \frac { 2 } { a _ { \delta } ^ { \frac { 2 } { 4 } } } \left( i - k \right) ! \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r ^ { i } P _ { i } ^ { k } ( \sin \phi _ { p } ) \cos k \lambda _ { p } ~ d V ~ ; ~ i , k \ge 0 ~ , ~ k \le i } } \\ { { \displaystyle S _ { i k } = \frac { 2 } { a _ { \delta } ^ { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } } \left( i - k \right) ! \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r ^ { i } P _ { i } ^ { k } ( \sin \phi _ { p } ) \sin k \lambda _ { p } ~ d V ~ ; ~ i , k > 0 ~ , ~ k \le i ~ . } } \end{array}
$$

(Remember that pp. = p(rp)/M is the relative density as introduced in eqns. (3.83). The use of the equatorial radius ag of the Earth and the isolation of the factor $\frac { G M } { r }$ in the representation (3.150)for the Earth's gravitational potential function is soinewhat arbitrary.It has the advantage that the coefficients $C _ { i k }$ and $S _ { i k }$ become dimensionless. Another advantage of this scaling procedureresidesinthe factthat forLEOs,where $\begin{array} { r } { \frac { u _ { \delta } } { r } \approx 1 } \end{array}$ ,the coeffcients may be easily compared with each other. in particular with the main term $C _ { 0 0 } = 1$ ·

The index $i$ is called the degree, $k$ the order of the spherical harmonic function in the above notation. For the sake of completeness we mention that the Legendre functions,multiplied by either $\sin k \lambda$ or $\cos k \lambda$ , are called spherical functions; if multiplied in addition with $\frac { 1 } { r ^ { i + 1 } }$ they are called spherical harmonic functions (and they are solutions of the Laplace equation (3.147)).

It is worthwhile to calculate the first few low degree (and order) coefficients $C _ { i k }$ and $S _ { i k }$ of the development (3.150). It should be mentioned that, because of

$$
\sin k \lambda = 0 ~ , ~ \mathrm { f o r } ~ k = 0 ~ ,
$$

one may set

$$
S _ { i 0 } = 0 \ , \quad \mathrm { ~ f o r ~ } i = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots .
$$

Let us now calculate all terms up to degree $i = 1$ using the defining equations (3.152). In order to preserve generality the results will first be given w.r.t. to an arbitrary coordinate system, only in the last step it will be assumed that the coordinate system refers to the center of mass. The result for the orders zero and one are:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C _ { 0 0 } = \displaystyle \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r _ { p } ^ { 0 } d V = 1 } } \\ { { \displaystyle V _ { 5 } } } \\ { { C _ { 1 0 } = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 5 } } \displaystyle \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r _ { p } \sin \phi _ { p } d V = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 5 } } \displaystyle \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r _ { p _ { 3 } } d V = \frac { r _ { 3 } } { a _ { 5 } } = 0 } } \\ { { \displaystyle V _ { 5 1 } = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 6 } } \displaystyle \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r _ { p } \cos \phi _ { p } \cos \lambda _ { p } d V = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 5 } } \displaystyle \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r _ { p _ { 1 } } d V = \frac { r _ { 1 } } { a _ { 5 } } = 0 } } \\ { { \displaystyle V _ { 5 1 } = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 5 } } \displaystyle \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r \cos \phi _ { p } \sin \lambda _ { p } d V = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 5 } } \displaystyle \int \rho _ { p _ { r } } r _ { p _ { 2 } } d V = \frac { r _ { 2 } } { a _ { 6 } } = 0 ~ . } } \end{array}
$$

The equalities “= 0” hold, because the coordinate system refers to the Earth's center of mass. The term Coo, multiplied by M, is the total mass of the Earth, the coefficients C1o, C11 and S11 are the center of mass coordinates divided bv ax (expressed in units of the equatorial radius at).

In principle the origin of the coordinate system should coincide with the center of mass of the Earth (this is what we assumed so far). In practice, one has the difficulty that the center of mass of the Earth has to be determined from satellite geodetic observations relative to a polyhedron of observing sites on the Earth's crust. This implies that, with improving observation techniques and processing strategies, better and better estimates for the center of mass relative to the polyhedron of observing stations become available. If a new center of mass estimate becomes available. one has in principle two options:

(a) Coefficients $C _ { 1 0 } \neq 0 , \ C _ { 1 1 } \neq 0$ und $S _ { 1 1 } \neq 0$ are accepted, or, (b） $C _ { 1 0 } = C _ { 1 1 } = S _ { 1 1 } = 0$ is enforced, but then the coordinates of the entire polyhedron of observing sites have to be adapted in order to refer to the newly established center of mass.

In practice one often prefers option (a) - obviously there are more users of station coordinates than of potential coefficients.

The coefficients of second degree may all be expressed in terms of the elements of the inertia tensor. The developments are first given w.r.t. an arbitrary coordinate system; in the last step the result refers to the PAI-system.

$$
